# Preface {.unnumbered}

These Theoretical Lab-Practical  notes accompany the course of **ETE :: Temporal and Spatial Econometrics – Spatial Econometrics module**, a module of the Master in Data Science for Social Sciences at the Department of Social, Political and Territorial Sciences at University of Aveiro. This version: academic year 2025–26.

## How to use these notes

Each chapter maps to a hands-on lab session. The text is structured in three layers:

1. **Narrative** — conceptual explanations and methodological context, always visible.
2. **Outputs** — tables, maps, and statistical results produced by the code, always visible.
3. **Code** — the Python implementation behind each output, **hidden by default**.

To reveal the code behind any result, click **▶ Show code** beside it.
To toggle all code on or off at once, use the **`</> Code`** button in the top-right toolbar.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Recommended reading flow
Read the narrative, study the outputs, then expand the code only when you want to understand the implementation. This separation keeps the explanatory text clean while keeping the full reproducible workflow one click away.
:::

## Session overview

| Session | Topic | Key methods |
|---------|-------|-------------|
|  | |
| **Topic 2** | MAUP, Spatial Weights & Autocorrelation | Weight matrices, Moran's I, LISA, diagnostics |
| **Topics 3–4** | Spatial Econometric Models | SLM, SEM, SDM, impacts, model selection |

## Software environment

All notebooks run on the `geomodelling` Python kernel. Core packages include:

`geopandas` · `libpysal` · `esda` · `spreg` · `splot` · `statsmodels` · `scikit-learn` · `osmnx` · `contextily`

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Reproducibility note
The HTML book is rendered from pre-executed notebooks — outputs are frozen and the kernel is **not** re-run during the build. To reproduce results locally, open the `.ipynb` files and run them with the `geomodelling` kernel active.
:::
